en.extract:
The western reef heron (Egretta gularis), also called the western reef egret, is a medium-sized heron found in southern Europe, Africa and parts of Asia. It has a mainly coastal distribution and occurs in several plumage forms: a slaty-grey plumage which can only be confused with the rather uncommon dark morph of the little egret (Egretta garzetta); a white form which can look very similar to the little egret, although the reef heron's bill tends to be paler and larger; and a black form with a white throat, E. g. gularis, found in West Africa. There are also differences in size, structure and foraging behaviour. There have been suggestions that the species hybridizes with the little egret, and based on this, some authors treat schistacea and gularis as subspecies of Egretta garzetta. Works that consider the western reef heron as a valid species include the nominate gularis and schistacea as subspecies.


Description
This bird has two plumage colour forms. There is an all-white morph and a dark grey morph; morphs can also occur with intermediate shades of grey which may be related to age or particoloured in grey and white. The white morph is similar in general appearance to the little egret, but has a larger yellower bill, extended yellow on thicker legs, and when foraging tends to be very active, sometimes also moving its wing or using it to shade the water surface. The grey morph has a whitish throat and is unlikely to be confused with any other species within the range of this egret with beak and legs similar to that of the white morph. During the breeding season the legs and facial skin are reddish. Breeding birds have two long feathers on the sides of the nape. The nominate subspecies gularis has a range from West Africa to Gabon, with some birds breeding in southern Europe. Subspecies schistacea (Hemprich & Ehrenberg, 1828) breeds from the Persian Gulf along the coast of India to the east of the India Peninsula. The bill of gularis is more pointed while schistacea has the larger bill especially towards the base.  The form on the eastern coast of South Africa is usually separated as the dimorphic egret Egretta dimorpha. The dark and white morph is thought to be controlled by a single allele with the dark character being incompletely dominant over the gene for white.


Taxonomy and systematics
The species was first described as Ardea gularis from a specimen obtained in Senegal by the French naturalist Louis Augustin Guillaume Bosc in 1792. The distinctive white throat (or gular region) in the dark morph gives it the species name. Later authors have treated it the genera Demiegretta (in which dimorphic egrets were once included), Herodias and Lepterodius until its current stable position in the genus Egretta. The distinctness of this species from Egretta garzetta has however been highly debated with some authors treating garzetta as a polytypic superspecies. There has been confusion related to what were thought to be dark little egrets and the possibility of hybrids. Dark morphs in little egrets are extremely rare and nearly all specimens of what were thought to be dark morphs have turned out to be western reef herons. In India some research claimed circumstantial evidence of interbreeding with little egrets, but the same researchers noted that the breeding seasons of the two species were different. Hybridization has also been claimed to occur in Morocco and Kenya (but this might refer to Egretta dimorpha). Christidis and Boles quote a report by McCracken and Sheldon (2002) that the nucleotide sequences of the cytochrome b genes from a sampled little egret and a western reef heron were identical and use this as evidence for demotion. The sequences and the origins of the samples are not publicly available or verifiable. In the past the Indian form which was also described as Ardea asha by William Henry Sykes has been treated as a subspecies of the little egret as Egretta garzetta schistacea on the basis of presumed hybridization with Egretta gularis. The lores of breeding little egrets are blue while those of the reef heron are reddish although some individuals of schistacea show blue and this is thought to be due to hybridization. The dark coastal form of Madagascar, Aldabra, Comoro Islands, Seychelles and parts of East Africa (southern Kenya to Tanzania) was earlier treated as a subspecies (the mainland African form has sometimes been considered as schistacea) but is now raised to full species as the dimorphic egret (Egretta dimorpha).


Distribution and status
It occurs mainly on the coasts in tropical west Africa, the Red Sea, the Persian Gulf (Iran) extending east to India. It also occurs in the Lakshadweep Islands and Sri Lanka where breeding was once recorded at Chilaw. The nominate subspecies breeds in west Africa from Mauritania to Gabon. Birds may also be found off the mainland such as in the Canary Islands. 
Small numbers breed in Spain. Subspecies schistacea is found from the Red Sea coast east round the Indian coast. Breeding colonies are known from the east coast of India around Pulicat Lake. They occasionally occur further inland.
The western reef heron (nominate subspecies) occurs as a vagrant in North America, South America and the Caribbean islands. Based on the growing number of records it is suspected that they may establish breeding colonies in Brazil. Several records around 1980–90 in Germany, Austria and France have been attributed to birds that escaped from an animal dealer in Mittelfranken.


Behaviour and ecology
These birds stalk their prey in shallow water, often running or stirring the water with their feet or flicking their wings to disturb prey; they may also stand still and wait to ambush prey. They eat fish, crustaceans, and molluscs. In coastal areas, they regularly feed on mudskippers. Laboratory studies show them to be capable of making corrections for refractions but the probability of missing increases when they are forced to strike at prey at very acute angles to the water surface. Like other herons and egrets they have few vocalizations, making a low kwok or grating sounds when disturbed or near the nest.
The western reef heron's breeding habitat is coastal wetlands. In the Red Sea region the breeding season is from June to August. The nominate subspecies breeds in West Africa from late April to September. In India the breeding season is during the monsoon rains from April to August and end of May in the former breeding colony at Chilaw in Sri Lanka. Most of the breeding colonies in Gujarat in western India were on mangroves. They nest in colonies usually of their own species but sometimes with other egrets and smaller herons, usually on platforms of sticks placed in trees or shrubs. The male fetches sticks of the nest while the female places them to form the platform. The nest height varies from about 5 to 15 meters, but very low nesting (as low as 0.6 meters) has been observed in mangrove trees in salt pans. The usual clutch is three to four eggs which are light blue as in the little egret. Incubation begins when the first egg is laid resulting in chicks with size disparities. Both parents take turns to incubate and the eggs hatch after about 23 to 24 days. The chicks are white with grey dapples. Young birds sometimes die after falling off from the nest platform. Adults will shade the chicks during the hotter parts of the day. Predators of eggs include rats and young chicks may be taken by cats and crows. Adults usually guard the nest when eggs or young chicks are present. Adults feed the young by regurgitating semi-digested food at the nest. This diet includes mainly small fishes of the families Clupeidae, Gobiidae and Engraulidae. The young birds leave the nest after about a month.


Gallery


References


External links

IUCN Heron specialist group (treated under Egretta garzetta)
Sound recordings on Xeno-Canto

The '''western reef heron''' ('''''Egretta gularis'''''), also called the '''western reef egret''', is a medium-sized [[heron]] found in southern Europe, Africa and parts of Asia. It has a mainly coastal distribution and occurs in several [[plumage]] forms: a [[slaty]]-grey plumage which can only be confused with the rather uncommon dark [[Polymorphism (biology)|morph]] of the [[little egret]] (''Egretta garzetta''); a white form which can look very similar to the little egret, although the reef heron's bill tends to be paler and larger; and a black form with a white throat, ''E. g. gularis'', found in West Africa. There are also differences in size, structure and foraging behaviour. There have been suggestions that the species hybridizes with the little egret, and based on this, some authors treat ''schistacea'' and ''gularis'' as subspecies of ''Egretta garzetta''. Works that consider the western reef heron as a valid species include the nominate ''gularis'' and ''schistacea'' as [[subspecies]].

==Description==

This [[bird]] has two plumage colour forms. There is an all-white morph and a dark grey morph; morphs can also occur with intermediate shades of grey which may be related to age<ref>{{cite journal|author=Dharmakumarsinhji, K.S. |year=1984|title=Some notes on the Indian Reef Heron|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume= 81|issue=1|pages=188–189 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48874096}}</ref> or particoloured in grey and white. The white morph is similar in general appearance to the [[little egret]], but has a larger yellower bill, extended yellow on thicker legs, and when foraging tends to be very active, sometimes also moving its wing or using it to shade the water surface. The grey morph has a whitish throat and is unlikely to be confused with any other species within the range of this [[egret]] with beak and legs similar to that of the white morph. During the breeding season the legs and facial skin are reddish.<ref name=softparts>{{cite journal|last1=Parasharya|first1=B.M.|last2=Naik|first2=R.M.|year=1987|title=Changes in the soft part coloration of the Indian Reef Heron, ''Egretta gularis'' (Bosc) related to age and breeding status |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. | volume=84 |issue=1 |pages=1–6|url= https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49081090}}</ref> Breeding birds have two long feathers on the sides of the nape. The nominate subspecies ''gularis'' has a range from West Africa to Gabon, with some birds breeding in southern Europe. Subspecies ''schistacea'' (Hemprich & Ehrenberg, 1828) breeds from the Persian Gulf along the coast of India to the east of the India Peninsula. The bill of ''gularis'' is more pointed while ''schistacea'' has the larger bill especially towards the base.  The form on the eastern coast of South Africa is usually separated as the [[dimorphic egret]] ''Egretta dimorpha''.<ref name=britbirds /><ref name=pcr>{{cite book|last1= Rasmussen|first1= P.C.|last2= Anderton|first2= J.C. |year= 2005| title=Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide. Volume 2|page=55|publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions|place=Washington DC and Barcelona}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Parasharya|first1=B.M.|last2=Naik|first2=R.M.|year=1984| title=The juvenile plumage of the Little Egret compared with that of the White-phase Indian Reef Heron|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=81| issue=3|pages=693–695|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48874659}}</ref> The dark and white morph is thought to be controlled by a single [[allele]] with the dark character being incompletely dominant over the gene for white.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Naik|first1=R.M.|last2=Parasharya|first2=B.M.|year=1983| title= Sequence of plumage changes and polymorphism in the Indian Reef Heron, ''Egretta gularis''| journal=Sandgrouse |volume=5|pages=75–81}}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The species was [[Species description|first described]] as ''Ardea gularis'' from a specimen obtained in Senegal by the French naturalist [[Louis Augustin Guillaume Bosc]] in 1792. The distinctive white throat (or gular region) in the dark morph gives it the species name. Later authors have treated it the genera ''Demiegretta'' (in which dimorphic egrets were once included<ref>{{cite journal|author=Berlioz, J.|year=1956| title= The dimorphic egrets|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=54|issue=1|pages=188–190| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48182830}}</ref>), ''Herodias'' and ''Lepterodius'' until its current stable position in the genus ''Egretta''. The distinctness of this species from ''Egretta garzetta'' has however been highly debated with some authors treating ''garzetta'' as a polytypic [[superspecies]]. There has been confusion related to what were thought to be dark little egrets and the possibility of hybrids. Dark morphs in little egrets are extremely rare and nearly all specimens of what were thought to be dark morphs have turned out to be western reef herons.<ref name=aquila>{{cite journal|last1=Magyar|first1=G.|last2=Yésou|first2=P.|year=2000| title=Reconsideration of a Hungarian specimen of a black-coloured egret as Western Reef Egret (Egretta gularis)|journal=Aquila |volume=105-106|pages=35–40|url=http://epa.oszk.hu/01600/01603/00083/pdf/Aquila_EPA-01603_1998-1999_035-040.pdf}}</ref> In India some research claimed circumstantial evidence of interbreeding with little egrets, but the same researchers noted that the breeding seasons of the two species were different.<ref name=timing>{{cite journal| last1=Naik|first1=R.M.|last2=Parasharya|first2=B.M.|last3=Patel|first3=B.H.|last4=Mansuri|first4=A.P.|year=1981|title=The timing of breeding season and interbreeding between the colour phases in the Indian Reef Heron ''Egretta gularis'' (Bosc)|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=78|issue=3|pages=494–497| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48229032}}</ref> Hybridization has also been claimed to occur in Morocco<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Qninba|first1=A.|last2=Benhoussa|first2=A.|last3=Ibn Tattou|first3=M.|last4=El Idrissi Essougrati|first4=A.|first5=S. Ben|last5=Haj|first6=H. Rguibi|last6=Idrissi|year=2011|title=Cas probable d'hybridation Aigrette garzette Egretta garzetta x Aigrette des récifs Egretta gularis dans l'archipel d'Essaouira (Maroc).|journal=Alauda|volume=79|pages=241–242|url=http://moroccanbirds.webs.com/PDFs/Qninba2011_Hybridation-Aigrettes.pdf|language=French|archive-date=2015-07-16|access-date=2013-04-17|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150716155418/http://moroccanbirds.webs.com/PDFs/Qninba2011_Hybridation-Aigrettes.pdf}}</ref> and Kenya (but this might refer to ''Egretta dimorpha'').<ref>{{cite book|author1=Hancock, J |author2=Naik, RM |year=1986|title=Inter-breeding between the Little Egret and the Western Reef Heron in India, Africa and possibly Israel. Abstract No. 411.|publisher=19th International Ornithological Congress}}</ref> Christidis and Boles quote a report by McCracken and Sheldon (2002) that the nucleotide sequences of the [[cytochrome b]] genes from a sampled little egret and a western reef heron were identical and use this as evidence for demotion. The sequences and the origins of the samples are not publicly available or verifiable.<ref>{{cite book|first1=Les|last1=Christidis|first2=Walter E.|last2=Boles|title= Systematics and taxonomy of Australian birds|url=https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri|url-access=limited|publisher=CSIRO|year=2008|page=[https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri/page/n120 110]}}</ref> In the past the Indian form which was also described as ''Ardea asha'' by [[William Henry Sykes]] has been treated as a subspecies of the little egret as ''Egretta garzetta schistacea'' on the basis of presumed hybridization with ''Egretta gularis''. The [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] of breeding little egrets are blue while those of the reef heron are reddish although some individuals of ''schistacea'' show blue and this is thought to be due to hybridization.<ref name=softparts /> The dark coastal form of Madagascar, Aldabra, Comoro Islands, Seychelles and parts of East Africa (southern Kenya to Tanzania) was earlier treated as a subspecies (the mainland African form has sometimes been considered as ''schistacea'') but is now raised to full species as the dimorphic egret (''Egretta dimorpha'').<ref name=britbirds>{{cite journal|journal=British Birds|title=Identification of western reef egrets and dark Little Egrets|first1=Philippe J. |last1=Dubois |first2=Pierre |last2=Yésou|volume=88|pages=307–319|year=1995|url=http://www.britishbirds.co.uk/search?model=pdf&id=7832}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title=A further note on the Relationship, Status, and Distribution of ''Egretta garzetta'', ''E. gularis'', ''E. schistacea'' and ''E. dimorpha''|pages=24–26|url=https://archive.org/stream/bulletinofbritis59tayl#page/24/mode/2up/|journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club|volume=59|issue=417|year=1938|author1=Grant, CHG  |author2=Mackworth-Praed, CW |name-list-style=amp }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Grant, CHB  |author2=C. W. Mackworth-Praed |name-list-style=amp |pages=189–196|url=https://archive.org/stream/bulletinofbritis53tayl#page/188/mode/2up/|volume=53|title=On the Relationship, Status and Range of Egretta garzetta, Demiegretta gularis, D. schistacea, D. asha, and D. dimorpha, a new subspecies and the correct type-locality of Egretta garzetta| journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club|year=1933}}</ref>

==Distribution and status==

It occurs mainly on the coasts in tropical west [[Africa]], the [[Red Sea]], the [[Persian Gulf]] (Iran)<ref>{{cite journal|title=Current Status of the Breeding Population of the Western Reef Heron Egretta gularis along the Northern Coasts of the Persian Gulf and Oman Sea, and its Wintering Population in the South of Iran| first1=Farzaneh|last1=Etezadifar|first2=Hamid| last2=Amini|url=http://www.wesca.net/Podoces/Podoces5.2/PODOCES%205_2_-%20Current%20status%20of%20Reef%20Heron%20in%20Iran.pdf|journal=Podoces| year=2010| volume=5|issue=2|pages=71–80}}</ref> extending east to [[India]]. It also occurs in the Lakshadweep Islands and [[Sri Lanka]] where breeding was once recorded at Chilaw.<ref name=hbk/> The nominate subspecies breeds in west Africa from Mauritania to Gabon. Birds may also be found off the mainland such as in the Canary Islands. 
Small numbers breed in Spain.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Dies |first1=J.I.|first2=J.|last2=Prosper|last3=Dies|first3=B.|year= 2001| title=Occasional breeding by Western Reef Egret in eastern Spain|journal= British Birds |volume=94|pages=382–386|url=http://www.birdingalbufera.es/files/recursos/ficheros/Reproduccion_gularis00_pdf.pdf}}</ref> Subspecies ''schistacea'' is found from the Red Sea coast east round the Indian coast.<ref name=britbirds /> Breeding colonies are known from the east coast of India around [[Pulicat Lake]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Kirkpatrick, K.M.|year=1961|title= The Ashy Reef Heron, ''Egretta gularis'' (Bosc) on the East Coast|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=58|issue=1|page=275|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48229032}}</ref> They occasionally occur further inland.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Neavoll, George F.|year=1968| title= Occurrence of the Reef Heron [''Egretta gularis'' (Bosc.)] in Hyderabad District|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=65|issue=1|pages=215–216|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48067708}}</ref>

The western reef heron (nominate subspecies) occurs as a vagrant in North America, South America and the Caribbean islands.<ref>{{cite journal|title=First record of the Western Reef-heron (''Egretta gularis'') for St. Vincent and the Grenadines|author=Paice, Michael R.|journal=Journal of Caribbean Ornithology|volume=19|issue=1|year=2006|url=http://www2.puc.edu/Faculty/Floyd_Hayes/jco/pdf/JCO%2019(1)%202006.pdf|pages=46–48|archive-date=2014-02-01|access-date=2013-04-16|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140201231509/http://www2.puc.edu/Faculty/Floyd_Hayes/jco/pdf/JCO%2019(1)%202006.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Murphy|first1=W. L.|last2=Nanan|first2= W.|year=1987|title=First confirmed record of Western Reef-Heron (Egretta gularis) for South America|journal=American Birds|volume=41|pages=16–27}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal   | last = Davis Jr.   | first = William E.  |  title = Foraging Behavior of a Western Reef Heron in North America   | journal = Colonial Waterbirds  | volume = 8  | issue = 1  | pages = 70–73  | year = 1985  | doi = 10.2307/1521198  | jstor=1521198}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title=Trans-Atlantic vagrancy of Palearctic birds in Trindidad and Tobago|last1=Kenefick|first1=Martyn|first2=Floyd E.|last2=Hayes|journal=J. Carib. Ornithol.|volume=19|pages=61–72|year=2006|url=http://www2.puc.edu/Faculty/Floyd_Hayes/jco/pdf/JCO%2019(2)%202006.pdf|archive-date=2014-02-01|access-date=2013-04-16|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140201231651/http://www2.puc.edu/Faculty/Floyd_Hayes/jco/pdf/JCO%2019(2)%202006.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Wilson, A. |year=2007|title=Western Reef-Heron (Egretta gularis gularis) at Calvert Vaux Park, Brooklyn|journal=Kingbird |volume=57|issue=4|pages=288–294}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Cardillo|first1=R.|first2= A. |last2=Forbes-Watson |first3=R. |last3=Ridgely|year=1983|title=The Western Reef–Heron (Egretta gularis) at Nantucket Island, Massachusetts|journal=American Birds |volume=37|issue=5|pages=827–829}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title=Notes on the Occurrence of the little egret (Egretta garzetta) in the Americas, with Reference to Other Palearctic Vagrants|first=William L. |last=Murphy|journal=Colonial Waterbirds|volume=15| issue=1 |year=1992|pages=113–123|doi=10.2307/1521360|jstor=1521360 }}</ref> Based on the growing number of records it is suspected that they may establish breeding colonies in Brazil.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Western Reef-Heron Egretta gularis in Brazil (Ciconiiformes: Ardeidae) |journal=Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia |volume=15|issue=3|pages=481–483 |first1=Carmem E.|last1=Fedrizzi|first2=Caio J.|last2=Carlos|first3=Teodoro |last3=Vaske Jr.|first4=Leandro |last4=Bugoni|first5=Danielle |last5=Viana|first6=Dráusio P. |last6=Véras|url=http://www.avibirds.com/pdf/W/Westelijke%20Rifreiger5.pdf}}</ref> Several records around 1980–90 in Germany, Austria and France have been attributed to birds that escaped from an animal dealer in Mittelfranken.<ref name=aquila/>

==Behaviour and ecology==

These birds stalk their prey in shallow water, often running or stirring the water with their feet or flicking their wings to disturb prey; they may also stand still and wait to ambush prey. They eat [[fish]], [[crustacean]]s, and [[mollusc]]s. In coastal areas, they regularly feed on [[mudskipper]]s.<ref name=hbk/> Laboratory studies show them to be capable of making corrections for refractions but the probability of missing increases when they are forced to strike at prey at very acute angles to the water surface.<ref>{{Cite journal|journal=Journal of Comparative Physiology A | year=1989|volume=165|pages=573–576|title=Stationary underwater prey missed by reef herons, Egretta gularis: head position and light refraction at the moment of strike|first1=Gadi |last1=Katzir|first2= Arnon |last2=Lotem|first3=Nathan |last3=Intrator|url=http://www.tau.ac.il/~lotem/Katzir%20et%20al%2089%20JCompPhysiolA.pdf|doi=10.1007/BF00611243|issue=4| s2cid=6191096}}</ref> Like other herons and egrets they have few vocalizations, making a low ''kwok'' or grating sounds when disturbed or near the nest.<ref name=hbk/>

The western reef heron's breeding habitat is coastal wetlands. In the Red Sea region the breeding season is from June to August. The nominate subspecies breeds in West Africa from late April to September. In India the breeding season is during the [[monsoon]] rains from April to August and end of May in the former breeding colony at Chilaw in Sri Lanka. Most of the breeding colonies in Gujarat in western India were on mangroves.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Naik|first1=R.M.|last2=Parasharya|first2=B.M.|year=1987| title=Impact of the food availability, nesting-habitat destruction and cultural variations of human settlements on the nesting distribution of a coastal bird, ''Egretta gularis'', in Western India|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=84|issue=2|pages=350–360|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49081409}}</ref> They nest in colonies usually of their own species but sometimes with other egrets and smaller herons, usually on platforms of sticks placed in trees or shrubs. The male fetches sticks of the nest while the female places them to form the platform. The nest height varies from about 5 to 15 meters, but very low nesting (as low as 0.6 meters) has been observed in mangrove trees in salt pans. The usual clutch is three to four eggs which are light blue as in the little egret. Incubation begins when the first egg is laid resulting in chicks with size disparities. Both parents take turns to incubate and the eggs hatch after about 23 to 24 days. The chicks are white with grey dapples. Young birds sometimes die after falling off from the nest platform. Adults will shade the chicks during the hotter parts of the day. Predators of eggs include rats and young chicks may be taken by cats and crows. Adults usually guard the nest when eggs or young chicks are present. Adults feed the young by regurgitating semi-digested food at the nest. This diet includes mainly small fishes of the families [[Clupeidae]], [[Gobiidae]] and [[Engraulidae]].<ref>{{cite journal|first1=Farzaneh |last1=Etezadifar |first2=Ahmad |last2=Barati |year=2011|title=Nestling diet of the Western Reef Heron, Egretta gularis, in Hara Biosphere Reserve, Persian Gulf|journal=Zoology in the Middle East|volume=54|issue=1|pages=131–133|doi=10.1080/09397140.2011.10648885|s2cid=83867378 }}</ref> The young birds leave the nest after about a month.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|pages=75–76|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 2.|edition=2nd|publisher=Oxford University Press|place=New Delhi|last1=Ali|first1=Salim|last2=Ripley| first2=S. Dillon|year=1978}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Tere, Anika |year=2004|title=Nesting of Western Reef-Egret Egretta gularis in the saltpans of G.H.C.L., Dholera |journal=Newsletter for Ornithologists|volume= 1|issue=5|pages=73–74}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Parasharya|first1=B.M.|last2=Naik|first2=R.M.|year=1988| title= Breeding biology of the Indian Reef heron| journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=85|issue=2|pages=251–262|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48804784}}</ref>

zh.extract:
黄喉岩鷺（學名：Egretta gularis）是一种中型个頭的鷺，分布在歐洲南部，非洲和亞洲部分地區。它主要生活在沿海，有兩種形式的羽毛，一种是石板灰色，另一种是白色，和小白鷺很像，只是它的喙更黯淡和大一些。除了羽毛顏色外，其大小、结構和覓食行為均有差異。
